lecture_context.lecture_title: Stanford CS224N: NLP w/ DL | Spring 2024 | Lecture 7 - Attention, Final Projects and LLM Intro

unit.name: Why Attention Fixes the Seq2Seq Bottleneck
unit.description: Motivates attention in encoder-decoder translation and walks through the core decoder-time-step attention procedure before introducing the main equations.
unit.summary: This segment motivates attention as a fix for the seq2seq bottleneck where an encoder must compress an entire source sentence into a single hidden state before decoding [ts=793s] [ts=833s]. The lecturer argues that this is implausible for longer sentences and unlike human translation, where people repeatedly look back at the source as they produce target words [ts=856s] [ts=874s]. He then describes the decoding procedure: compare the current decoder state to each encoder state, turn the resulting scores into a softmax distribution over source positions, compute a weighted average of encoder states, and combine that context vector with the decoder hidden state to predict the next word [ts=935s] [ts=1019s]. The discussion closes by formalizing dot-product scoring, softmax-normalized attention weights, and context-vector concatenation before highlighting attention’s performance, interpretability, and gradient-flow benefits [ts=1146s] [ts=1401s].

key_points.text: The original encoder-decoder setup forces all useful source information into one hidden vector.
Attention lets the decoder look back at different source positions as needed during generation.
Attention scores are turned into a softmax distribution over encoder positions.
The attention output is a weighted average of encoder hidden states.
The decoder concatenates the context vector with its hidden state before predicting the next word.
Attention provides interpretability by showing which source words the model focuses on at each decoding step.
